item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
She was starting at its face

Should that be 'staring'?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Have never had previous issues with … major infectious disease

That's a bit vague. Does that mean anything more serious than the common cold? Or does non-major also include things like measles, chicken pox, etc?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's valid enough criticism. I wrestled with the containment class for a while. Surprised you're the first one to bring it up. I thought it'd be everyone's first objection. Thus, the well-thought-out rebuttal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, I suppose it's somewhat self-containing in that regard. Isn't that Heimal lol All right, consider that criticism revoked.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fixed.

And, they may not understand it, they may not be able to stop it- but they can silence the after-effects pretty easily. Just a bigger conceptual box. It doesn't hurt people; it doesn't cause tons of property damage; it doesn't recur in a given location; it doesn't leave anomalous evidence behind- it's a one-time event that's easily identified and suppressed, witnessed only by a small, localized group of people every couple years. Even if they miss one, who's going to believe it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Creepy and weird in a good way. But this has got to be Keter. There's no way to prevent this happening. Also, 'phenomena' is plural.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A lot of skips these days have well-defined mythology, tons of backstory, historical context. Which is fun, and adds to the Skip, about 90% of the time. But I think we should be able to write about purely inexplicable phenomena. I'm rather hoping that the trend of 'longer, more in depth, more meta, more format-screwy, more exposition-heavy' work on the site will give way. I don't want it to go away; but I'd like the site writing culture to embrace simplicity as much as complexity. Taking the best of the first series, and the last.

Also: very deliberately didn't even hint at this, but my headcanon is that it's a hive-mind amalgamation of completely mundane vermin, using something anomalous to launch 'Arks' of noisome critters into human habitations. Sort of a, 'whole species responding to human encroachment as an aggregate whole' thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed. Very retro, in a good way. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm super into this. It's simple, it's inexplicable, it's horrifying; the fact that it's traumatic and messy and defies consensus reality without being especially dangerous in and of itself is refreshing. There's just enough here to get my imagination going about what could be causing this - is it phantom overlay from another universe where things have gone very wrong? An effort by some kind of rodent god to make people of its own? Just a nonsensical glitch in reality? I love the old-school feel here of a memorable, stimulating, simple-yet-baffling weird thing.

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded: It was meant to be a short, simple example of Cronenbergian fiction. A couple people have mentioned they'd like more explanation, but I don't think every skip needs a mythos behind it. Sometimes, horrifying inexplicable things happen. Maybe it has an explanation: but the Foundation will never know it.

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded:
True enough- but if someone who said they were from the CDC said you needed a shot after being thumped with a sack of bloody rats, you'd probably be like, 'yeah, sounds reasonable.' Someone tries to make you take an injection or a pill at gunpoint, or pulls out aerosols and starts shooting them around, you might struggle, duck the thing, and escape with full knowledge of something the Man doesn't want you to know. That's my take, anyway. Maybe in-universe, the Foundation pushes routine batteries of public health inoculations harder than we do here, to make it more mundane.

EDIT: Ninja'd

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded:
Some people are more suspicious than others, but I don't think convincing people to have injections is that hard if you have the right trappings of authority, since there are plenty of more-or-less realistic situations where it's appropriate ('we're from the CDC, your neighbour has meningococcal disease, as a contact you should get a booster vaccination')

Minor nitpick with the article on that subject - rats don't transmit rabies, although since it's a lie anyway and the character probably wouldn't be expected to know that I can easily overlook it.

Tentatively upvoted - this is pretty simple, and by the end of the article I was left kind of disappointed wanting to know more about what's going on here, but I only cared enough to feel that way since what was presented was solid, classic body horror.

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded:
I'm not so sure.

I haven't had an injection outside of a local anesthetic for dental work since I was a teenager.

If someone tried to give me a "routine injection" i'd be like "…routine for what?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: feels really series 1, it’s nice, not my favorite but it’s still really off. 1+

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded: I imagine administering amnestics via coercion or just spraying a load of chemicals in someone's face would be more difficult and risky than just posing as authority and giving someone a 'routine shot.' And, I imagine after the hundredth time you order a civilian mind-wiped, you get a bit blasé about the whole thing.

A lot of people have done the whole "infestation/disease" and "monster exploding out of someones torso" horror schtick before, but something about this really struck me. I think it was the interview log; something about it felt very realistic and personal, and I enjoyed the casual-ness of the administering of the amnestic.
